ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mochi-filled black sesame xiao long bao. FOODON_03315595 (dumpling food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Dumplings, filled pasta) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for mochi-skin sweet soup dumplings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dessert dumpling product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 10 pieces, estimated at approximately 200 g total, as sold by Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous rice dumpling with black sesame filling — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and peer-reviewed composition of black sesame paste and mochi dough
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) with component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for Din Tai Fung Black Sesame Mochi Xiao Long Bao. Values are estimated by component analysis: mochi skin (glutinous rice flour, water, wheat starch) weighted at approximately 55% of total mass, black sesame paste filling (black sesame seeds, sugar, lard) at approximately 45%. Reference data drawn from USDA SR Legacy entries: Glutinous rice flour (FDC ID 169717), Black sesame seeds (FDC ID 169414), and Sugar (FDC ID 169655). Energy and macros are blended proportionally. Sodium is low as no added salt is typical in this dessert product. Values represent per-100 g of the finished steamed product.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice-based products — GI analogous estimate based on Tang Fook Yuen et al. and University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice and sweet rice dumplings
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Sesame Mochi Xiao Long Bao. GI estimated at 65 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice products (GI range 60–98 depending on preparation and filling). Mochi skin is predominantly glutinous rice flour, which has a moderate-to-high GI; the black sesame and sugar filling moderates the overall GI slightly. A conservative mid-range estimate of 65 (medium GI) is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 87.0) / 100 = 56.6 g, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 43.5 × 2 = 87.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g; ~110 mg per 10-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 110 mg sodium per 10-piece serving, Black Sesame Mochi Xiao Long Bao poses minimal sodium burden for individuals managing hypertension, though portion control remains advisable given the moderate energy density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~57 per serving) driven by glutinous rice flour skin and added sugar in filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glutinous rice mochi skin and sweetened black sesame filling produce a high glycemic load of approximately 57 per 10-piece serving, making this product unsuitable for routine consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g per 100 g) from lard or shortening in filling, partially offset by beneficial polyunsaturated fats from black sesame
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black sesame seeds contribute polyunsaturated fatty acids and plant sterols that may be cardioprotective, but the saturated fat from lard or shortening in the filling warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, particularly when consumed in a 10-piece serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Sesame Mochi Xiao Long Bao is a dessert variant of the classic Shanghai soup dumpling (xiao long bao), distinguished by its chewy mochi skin made from glutinous rice flour rather than the traditional wheat-based wrapper. Each piece is filled with a rich, nutty black sesame paste sweetened with sugar and enriched with lard or vegetable shortening, then steamed to order. The result is a soft, yielding exterior with a dense, aromatic filling that delivers an intense roasted sesame flavour. This product is a signature dessert offering at Din Tai Fung, the internationally acclaimed Taiwanese-origin restaurant chain with outlets across Singapore. Nutritionally, each 10-piece serving (approximately 200 g) provides around 570 kcal, 87 g of carbohydrate, and 20 g of fat, with a moderate sodium content of approximately 110 mg. The glutinous rice skin gives the product a medium-to-high glycemic index (estimated GI 65), resulting in a high glycemic load per serving (~57), which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Black sesame seeds are a notable source of calcium, iron, magnesium, and polyunsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to the use of lard in traditional preparations. It contains sesame allergens and trace gluten from wheat starch used in the mochi skin. In Singapore, this dish is served as a dessert or sweet dim sum item and is popular across Chinese-speaking communities.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Din Tai Fung Black Sesame Mochi Xiao Long Bao exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for glutinous rice flour, black sesame seeds, and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice products. Confidence is medium because the component proportions are estimated from product description and general knowledge of this dish type rather than laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data (glutinous rice flour FDC ID 169717, black sesame seeds FDC ID 169414, sugar FDC ID 169655) as no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific product. Serving size of 200 g for 10 pieces is estimated based on typical Din Tai Fung portion weights for dessert XLB; actual weight may vary. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — no hydrogenated fats are used in this product type). 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — black sesame and glutinous rice contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data). 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources for this product type. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component source data used for estimation. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived B12 source (lard contributes negligible B12); not reported in component sources. 7. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 10. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for glutinous rice products; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 87.0) / 100 = 56.6, rounded to 56.6. 11. Halal status is uncertain — Din Tai Fung Singapore outlets are not universally halal-certified; lard may be used in some preparations. is_halal set to false as a conservative default. 12. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dessert dumpling variant.
